metadata.summary: Structured 6-step process for evaluating options against weighted criteria with sensitivity analysis to distinguish robust winners from statistical noise.

content.title: Decision Analysis
content.summary: Structured 6-step process for evaluating options against weighted criteria: define decision → identify options → define and lock criteria with weights → score options with evidence URLs per cell → sensitivity analysis (±20% weight Monte Carlo) → document rationale. Weights are locked before scoring; sensitivity analysis determines whether the recommendation is robust or fragile (top option wins less than 70% of Monte Carlo trials → escalate to human).

why.p: Decisions made by gut feel cannot be audited, re-examined, or calibrated over time. Making criteria weights explicit collapses argument-by-anecdote into argument-by-number. The sensitivity analysis surface is the key step that the README omits: a 3.85 vs 3.70 weighted total is well within rater noise — without Monte Carlo you cannot distinguish a real winner from statistical noise. The decision artifact in `.aidocs/` lets future teams audit and improve calibration.

list.item: Reversible-but-expensive choice with three or more candidate options where the team is sliding toward gut feel (CRM selection, build-vs-buy, LLM provider choice).
Stakeholders disagree because they secretly weight criteria differently — making weights explicit collapses the argument.
Decision will be re-litigated later (board review, audit, post-mortem) and a written rationale is needed.
Comparing N options against a current baseline (Pugh matrix mode).
A decision has long-tail risk that only surfaces when tabulated (vendor lock-in, regulatory exposure).
Sequential / conditional decisions with probabilities — switch to decision tree with expected value.
Two-option, low-cost, easily reversible decisions — use a 5-minute pros/cons list.
Decision is actually about strategy, not selection — run a brainstorm session first.
Analysis is retrofitted to justify a decision already made (the #1 failure mode).
Pure financial trade-offs with quantifiable cash flows — use NPV / discounted cash flow directly.
Decisions under deep uncertainty where numbers have more than one order-of-magnitude error.

rule.statement: Lock criteria and weights before scoring any option. Record the weight-setter name and timestamp. No weight changes after scoring begins.
rule.rationale: Stakeholders who want a specific option will lobby to raise weights for criteria where it wins. Weights locked before scoring with a named author prevent post-hoc manipulation and make manipulation visible to reviewers.

rule.statement: Cap the criteria list at 7 items. More than 7 and stakeholders cannot hold them in working memory; weights become arbitrary.
rule.rationale: Cognitive load above 7 criteria produces weight assignments that do not reflect real priorities. The cap forces the BA to consolidate collinear criteria (cost + TCO + subscription price → total cost).

rule.statement: Check for collinear criteria before weights are set. If two criteria have correlation above 0.6 (e.g., "cost" and "TCO"), merge them. Collinearity secretly inflates one axis to 2× or 3× its stated weight.
rule.rationale: Collinearity is the most common invisible error in decision matrices. "Cost (25%) + TCO (20%) + subscription price (15%)" is really "cost 60%" in a matrix labelled as "cost 25%".

rule.statement: Every cell in the evaluation matrix must cite at least one evidence URL (vendor docs, pricing page, GitHub repo, measured uptime data). A score without a source is a vote, not data. Reject cells without evidence.
rule.rationale: LLMs hallucinate vendor capabilities and pricing confidently. Evidence URLs make claims falsifiable and enable post-decision calibration reviews.

rule.statement: Score evidence must be dated. Evidence older than 90 days must be re-fetched before use. Vendor pricing and uptime data stale quickly.
rule.rationale: A CRM priced at $50/seat in January may be $80/seat in September. Stale evidence produces wrong scores that compound into wrong recommendations.

rule.statement: Score one criterion across all options before moving to the next criterion. Never score one option fully then the next. Scoring per-criterion prevents anchoring on the first option scored.
rule.rationale: When one option is scored fully first, later options drift toward 3 (regression to median) because the first option becomes the implicit anchor. Per-criterion scoring breaks the anchor.

rule.statement: Define direction for every criterion: higher_better or lower_better. Score 5 = best for this criterion in every row. Never mix directions within a matrix. Include this field in the criteria definition.
rule.rationale: Mixing directions silently breaks the math: "cost: 5 = expensive" in one row and "cost: 5 = cheap" in another produces a nonsensical total. The criterion definition must encode direction before any scoring begins.

rule.statement: After scoring, run Monte Carlo sensitivity analysis with ±20% weight perturbation over 2000 trials. If the top option wins fewer than 70% of trials, the recommendation is fragile — escalate to human or gather more evidence before committing.
rule.rationale: A 3.85 vs 3.70 weighted total is well within rater noise (±0.5 on a 1-5 scale). Without sensitivity analysis, the "winner" may be statistical noise. The 70% threshold converts this judgment into a concrete go/no-go criterion.

rule.statement: Always include a "do nothing / status quo" option, even when it feels unnecessary. It calibrates the rest of the matrix and forces the team to state what they lose by stalling.
rule.rationale: Without a status-quo option, the matrix proves that one of the presented options is better than the others — but not that any of them is better than doing nothing.

example.title: CRM Selection Worked Example
example.code:
# Criteria (locked 2026-04-26, weight-setter: CEO) | Criterion | Weight | Direction | |-----------------|--------|-----------------| | Feature fit | 25% | higher_better | | Total cost 3yr | 25% | lower_better | | Ease of use | 20% | higher_better | | Integration | 15% | higher_better | | Support SLA | 15% | higher_better |
# Evidence per cell (sampled) Salesforce / Feature fit: score=5 / source: https://salesforce.com/features (fetched 2026-04-26) HubSpot / Total cost: score=4 / source: https://hubspot.com/pricing (fetched 2026-04-26)
# Weighted totals Salesforce: 3.55, HubSpot: 4.00, Pipedrive: 3.85
# Sensitivity analysis output (sensitivity.py) Robustness under ±20% weight noise: HubSpot: 81.4% mean=3.99 sd=0.18 ← winner, robust (>70%) Pipedrive: 11.3% mean=3.83 sd=0.20 Salesforce: 7.3% mean=3.53 sd=0.21
# Recommendation: HubSpot Rationale: Best feature/cost balance; wins 81% of sensitivity trials. Risk: Limited Salesforce-CRM migration support. Mitigation: pilot import with 500 contacts first.
example.note: Decision: Select CRM platform for a 12-person sales team with a $30k/year budget cap. Evidence URLs required for all cells.

example.title: Build vs Buy Example
example.code:
# Criteria (locked 2026-03-15, weight-setter: CTO) | Criterion | Weight | Direction | |--------------------|--------|----------------| | Time to market | 30% | lower_better | | Total cost 3yr | 25% | lower_better | | Customization need | 25% | higher_better | | Operational risk | 20% | lower_better |
# Scoring (evidence URLs required) | Criterion | Wt | Build | Buy (SQS) | Buy (CloudAMQP) | |---------------|----|-------|-----------|-----------------| | Time to mkt | 30%| 2 | 5 | 5 | | Cost 3yr | 25%| 3 | 4 | 4 | | Customization | 25%| 5 | 2 | 3 | | Op risk | 20%| 2 | 5 | 4 | | Weighted | | 3.00 | 4.05 | 4.05 |
# Sensitivity: SQS and CloudAMQP tied (>70% each under ±20% weight noise) # Escalate to human: choose by operational preference (existing AWS stack → SQS)
example.note: Decision: Build custom queue service or buy a managed queue. Criteria lock before scoring.

antipattern.description: Analysis performed to justify a decision already made: options are scored after the winner is known, and weights are set to produce the predetermined result.
antipattern.reason: Reviewers detect post-hoc rationalization from weight patterns that perfectly match the chosen option. Trust drops and the decision process loses legitimacy for future decisions.

antipattern.description: Pure financial trade-off scored on a 1-5 scale instead of using NPV or discounted cash flow.
antipattern.reason: A "cost" score of 3 vs 4 throws away precision that financial analysis preserves. For purely financial decisions, use NPV directly; reserve 1-5 scoring for non-quantifiable criteria.

rule.statement: Pass 1 (Frame pass, sonnet): reads decision context and emits the structured frame {decision_statement, objectives[], constraints[], decision_maker, criteria[]}. Weights are NOT assigned in this pass. Output JSON only.
rule.rationale: Separating frame from scoring prevents the most common failure: weights assigned by the same agent that generates options, which causes unconscious steering toward a preferred outcome.

rule.statement: Pass 2 (Score pass): for each option × criterion cell, a separate agent retrieves evidence (vendor docs, pricing pages, GitHub metrics) and emits {option, criterion, evidence_urls[], normalized_score_1_5, confidence, caveats[]}. Use multiple agents in parallel — one per criterion or one per option — to reduce single-agent bias. If evidence cannot be found, return confidence=low with empty evidence_urls; never hallucinate a score.
rule.rationale: A single agent scoring the entire matrix inherits its own biases across all cells. Parallel scoring agents with independent evidence retrieval produce more calibrated and disagreement-surfacing results.

rule.statement: Pass 3 (Sensitivity/dissent pass, opus): runs Monte Carlo over weights (±20%) and confidence intervals on cells, emits recommendation with robustness score and lists which cells, if flipped, would change the answer. If gap between #1 and #2 is less than 0.3, recommendation must be "tied — escalate to human".
rule.rationale: The dissent pass is the only pass that can override the apparent matrix winner. Its output is the actual decision recommendation; the matrix is supporting evidence, not the decision.

rule.statement: Three mandatory human-in-the-loop checkpoints: (1) weights before scoring starts, (2) final recommendation before commitment, (3) post-implementation calibration review (compare predicted scores to actual outcomes, 6-12 months later).
rule.rationale: Agents produce the matrix; humans own the decision and the calibration review. Without the calibration review, the team never learns whether its scoring rubrics were accurate, so quality never improves.

rule.statement: Archive the final matrix and rationale in .aidocs/ alongside the project's other artifacts. Future teams must be able to audit the decision and improve calibration from historical data.
rule.rationale: Decision quality improves when previous decisions are reviewed against outcomes. An unarchived decision cannot be audited, calibrated, or defended in a post-mortem.

section.name: limitations-and-failure-modes
section.p: False precision: a 3.85 vs 3.70 weighted total looks like a winner but is well within rater noise (typically ±0.5 on a 1-5 scale). Without sensitivity analysis the "winner" is often statistical noise.
Criteria collinearity: "cost" and "TCO" and "subscription price" are often the same axis triple-counted, secretly inflating financial weight to 60%+ while the table shows 25%.
Inverse scaling errors: cost is "lower is better" but features is "higher is better". Mixing direction silently breaks the math. Always normalize to "5 = best for this criterion".
Anchoring on first option: the option scored first becomes the reference; later options drift toward 3 (regression to mean). Score one criterion across all options before moving to the next.
Missing options: the matrix evaluates the options you brought; it cannot tell you the option you forgot. Pair with explicit divergence (brainstorm) before convergence (matrix).
Weight gaming: stakeholders who want option B will lobby to raise the weight of criteria where B wins. Lock weights *before* scoring; record who set them and when.
No-uncertainty assumption: the matrix treats each cell as a point estimate. Real options have wide ranges (cost ±50%, time ±100%); use Monte Carlo or ranges if stakes are high.

section.name: agentic-workflow-extended
section.p: Drive this with a three-pass structured pipeline. (1) **Frame pass** — a sonnet agent reads the decision context and emits the structured frame {decision_statement, objectives[], constraints[], decision_maker, criteria[]}; weights are NOT yet assigned. (2) **Score pass** — for each option × criterion cell, an agent retrieves evidence (vendor docs, pricing pages, GitHub issue counts, last-12-month uptime data) and emits {option, criterion, raw_evidence_url[], normalized_score_1_5, confidence}. Multiple agents in parallel reduce single-agent bias. (3) **Sensitivity / dissent pass** — an opus agent runs Monte Carlo over weights (±20%) and confidence intervals on cells, emits a recommendation with a "robustness score" and lists which cells, if flipped, would change the answer. Only the final memo is prose; everything else is JSON. The decision itself stays human — agents produce the matrix, the human signs.

rule.statement: Recommended subagents: brainstorm to diverge/converge/review and expand the option set before the matrix is locked. faion-sdd-executor-agent to generate the implementation SDD task tree once the recommended option is signed off. feature-executor to execute discrete tasks that fall out of the decision (vendor onboarding, contract signing, migration plan). improver to run quarterly: read decisions made 6-12 months ago, compare predicted scores to actual outcomes, log calibration errors as a recurring pattern. A custom decision-evidence-gatherer: input = {option, criterion}, output = {evidence_urls[], extracted_facts[], normalized_score, confidence}. Reuse across decisions; it caches vendor research. A custom criteria-deduplicator: input = criteria list, output = clusters of collinear criteria with merge recommendation. Catches the "cost / TCO / pricing" triple-count failure.
rule.rationale: Specialized agents reduce rework and improve calibration over time.

list.item: Lock weights *before* scoring options; record the weight-setter and timestamp. Otherwise weights drift to support a preferred answer.
Score one criterion across all options at once, not one option fully then the next — kills anchoring bias on the first option scored.
Always include a "do nothing" / status-quo option, even when it feels silly. It calibrates the rest of the matrix.
Force evidence URLs on every cell. A score with no source is a vote, not data.
Publish raters' individual scores before averaging — disagreement is signal; collapsing it into a mean hides it.
Run sensitivity analysis (±20% on weights, ±1 on scores) automatically. If the top changes, the decision is fragile, not robust.
Keep the matrix to ≤ 7 criteria. More than 7 and stakeholders cannot hold them in working memory; weights become arbitrary.
Pre-mortem the recommended option ("assume in 12 months this was the wrong call — why?"). The output goes into the Risks section, not as an afterthought.
Archive the final matrix + rationale in the project's `.aidocs/` (or equivalent) so the next team can audit and learn calibration.

antipattern.description: Agents will hallucinate vendor capabilities and pricing.
antipattern.reason: Force evidence URLs on every cell and reject cells without primary sources (vendor docs, pricing pages, GitHub repos), not blog posts.

antipattern.description: LLMs cluster in the middle of 1-5 scales (regression to median).
antipattern.reason: Calibrate by asking for percentile rank instead, or by anchoring with two extreme reference options.

antipattern.description: A single agent doing all scoring inherits its own biases across the whole matrix.
antipattern.reason: Use parallel scoring agents (one per criterion or one per option) and compare; large disagreement = low confidence cells.

antipattern.description: Agents will silently mix scoring directions ("cost: 5 = expensive" in one row, "5 = cheap" in another).
antipattern.reason: Fix at the schema level: every criterion has an explicit `direction: "higher_better"|"lower_better"`.

antipattern.description: Date drift: pricing and uptime data goes stale fast.
antipattern.reason: Stamp `retrieved_at` on every fact; agents must refuse to reuse evidence > 90 days old without re-fetching.

antipattern.description: LLMs love to recommend the option you mentioned first or last.
antipattern.reason: Randomize option order in the prompt before the score pass.
The matrix is decision *support*, not decision *making*. Human-in-the-loop checkpoints: weights (before scoring), final rec (before commitment), post-implementation calibration review.

antipattern.description: Watch for prompt-injected criteria: if option descriptions come from vendor websites, vendors can plant criteria favoring themselves.
antipattern.reason: Strip suspicious instructions; never let vendor copy define weights.

template.purpose: Full Decision Analysis Document: context, options, criteria/weights, evaluation matrix, sensitivity, recommendation, approval.
Lightweight matrix template for lower-stakes decisions.
Monte Carlo over ±20% weight perturbations; prints robustness % per option. Recommends escalation if winner is below 70%.
LLM prompt for evidence-backed cell scoring: evidence URLs required, confidence field, no-marketing-copy rule.
